title: Butterfly key repair for a split tabletop
subtitle: Glue the split, rout the recess, and drive the key across the grain
excerpt: How to stabilise a split or cracked tabletop with butterfly keys (bow-tie inlays): gluing the split closed, marking and chopping the recesses, fitting the key, and levelling the surface.
sourceNotes: Charles Hayward Cabinet Making and Finishing. Traditional woodworking and furniture restoration guides (public domain). Collins Complete DIY Manual.

content.text: A
butterfly key

does not close a split; it prevents it from extending or opening further. For a decorative hardwood table, the key is often made from a contrasting timber and left exposed as a feature. For an invisible repair, use the same species.
Wood movement
continues after the repair; do not glue the key tight if the split is a live seasonal gap.
Step 1, glue the split if fresh

If the split is a fresh break with clean mating edges (not a long-standing seasonal gap), apply wood glue into the split and clamp with sash cramps from beneath. Clean off squeeze-out immediately. Allow to cure fully (at least 12 hours) before cutting the butterfly recesses.
Step 2, cut the butterfly blanks

Cut butterfly blanks from hardwood at 6 to 8 mm thickness. A standard butterfly is approximately 80 mm from tip to tip and 30 mm across the waist, but scale to the split length. The key sits at right angles to the split and must cross it by at least 30 mm on each side.
Step 3, mark and chop the recess

Position each butterfly blank across the split and mark around it with a marking knife or a sharp pencil. Score the outline clearly. Chop straight down on the outline with a bevel-edge chisel and mallet, then pare out the waste in thin layers to the depth of the key (6 to 8 mm). Check the depth at all four sides: the key should sit flush with the surface. Pare the walls of the recess until the key slides in snugly with hand pressure only.
Step 4, fit and glue

Apply a thin coat of wood glue to the recess walls and the underside and sides of the butterfly key. Press the key home, ensuring it sits fully flush or fractionally proud (never below the surface). Clamp if needed with a G-cramp over a protective scrap block. Remove squeeze-out immediately.
Step 5, level and finish

Once the glue has cured, level any proud material with the card scraper held at a low angle, working across the grain of the key. Follow with 120-grit and 180-grit sandpaper on a block, feathering into the surrounding surface. Blend the finish: apply the same oil, wax, or polish as the surrounding table to blend the key area.
Common mistakes
Recess too deep.
A key glued below the surface is very hard to fix. Test the fit before any glue is applied; pare in very thin passes.
Key glued across an active seasonal gap.
If the table moves seasonally, a rigidly glued key will cause a new split adjacent to the recess. Dry-fit only, or glue only the centre contact, leaving the tips free to move.
Key grain running the wrong way.
The grain of the butterfly key should run across the split (perpendicular to the split line). A key with grain running parallel to the split provides no holding strength.
